output.final_deliverable:
1) Article: "Dozens of state attorneys general urge US Congress not to block AI laws | Reuters"
- Publication date: MISSING (not provided in the merged results)
- Main topic / key point: Based on the headline, dozens of state attorneys general are urging the U.S. Congress not to block state-level AI laws (i.e., pressure in favor of allowing AI regulation to proceed).
- Source website: Reuters
- URL: https://www.reuters.com/legal/litigation/dozens-state-attorneys-general-urge-us-congress-not-block-a

2) Article: "Backed by Anthropic, a Super PAC Group Begins an Ad Blitz in Support of A.I. Regulation"
- Publication date: 2026-02-23 (date present in the article URL in the merged results)
- Main topic / key point: A Super PAC backed by Anthropic has launched an advertising blitz to support A.I. regulation — showing industry-backed political campaigning in favor of regulatory action.
- Source website: The New York Times
- URL: https://www.nytimes.com/2026/02/23/technology/ai-pac-ad-blitz.html

Notes on missing evidence: The merged results provided headlines and URLs but did not include the full publication date or article text for the Reuters item; full article content, direct quotes, and detailed context for both pieces were not provided in the merged results.
output.action_summary:
Comparison of perspectives:
- Common theme: Both items indicate active support for A.I. regulation from influential actors — one from state legal officials and the other from an industry-backed political group.
- Differences in actors and tactics: The Reuters headline highlights collective legal/political pressure from public officials (dozens of state attorneys general) urging Congress not to block state AI laws. The New York Times item highlights private-sector political advocacy (a Super PAC backed by Anthropic) using paid advertising to push for regulation.
- Implications: Together these pieces suggest a cross-sector alignment (public-sector officials and some private-sector actors) pushing for regulatory outcomes on AI, but via different channels — legal/legislative pressure versus political advertising/campaign influence.
- Missing details for deeper comparison: full publication date and article body for the Reuters item, direct quotes, the scale and content of the Super PAC ads, any opposing viewpoints or Congressional responses, and whether the articles report coordination between these actors. The merged results did not include those details, so a more thorough comparison would require visiting the full articles (URLs provided).

76bf40af-14d1-463e-86c5-5b293a3f1e11.title: Search for recent news articles about 'artificial intelligence regulation' from the past year. Visit at least 2 different news websites (not Wikipedia) and extract: (1) The headline of each article, (2) The publication date, (3) The main topic or key point, (4) The source website name. Provide a summary comparing the different perspectives.

merged_results.title: Search news articles for "artificial intelligence regulation" (past year)

result.query: artificial intelligence regulation site:news OR site:nytimes.com OR site:reuters.com OR site:bbc.com OR site:theguardian.com OR site:washingtonpost.com "artificial intelligence regulation"
result.intent: Find recent (within the past 12 months) news articles about AI regulation from reputable news websites (exclude Wikipedia). Return top 6–8 results prioritized by relevance and recency.
vector_context: Synthesize extracted facts and compare perspectives (post-visit think step)

results.title: Dozens of state attorneys general urge US Congress not to block AI laws | Reuters
results.description: Nov 25 (Reuters) - Republican and Democratic attorneys general from 35 states and the District of Columbia urged congressional leaders on Tuesday not to block state laws governing artificial intelligence, warning of "disastrous consequences" if the technology is left unregulated. The letter sets up a clash between the states and the Trump administration over AI regulation, as the industry seeks to avoid new laws scheduled to take effect in 2026 and states worry about injuries and deaths attributed to chatbot use.

results.title: Backed by Anthropic, a Super PAC Group Begins an Ad Blitz in Support of A.I. Regulation - The New York Times
results.description: The advertising blitz is part of an escalating political war over A.I. in the run-up to the midterm elections. States and the federal government are at odds over how to regulate the technology. While President Trump has said A.I. regulation could slow down American companies and boost Chinese rivals, states are pushing ahead with their own laws.

results.title: Don’t be fooled. The US is regulating AI – just not the way you think | Sacha Alanoca and Maroussia Lévesque | The Guardian
results.description: Citing national security risks, the US controls the exports of the most advanced chips and, under Biden, even model weights – the “secret sauce” that turns user queries into results. These AI regulations are hiding in dense administrative language – “Implementation of Additional Export Controls” or “Supercomputer and Semiconductor End Use” bury the ledes.

results.title: Senate Votes to Allow State A.I. Laws, a Blow to Tech Companies - The New York Times
results.description: There are no federal laws regulating A.I. but states have enacted dozens of laws that strengthen consumer privacy, ban A.I.-generated child sexual abuse material and outlaw deepfake videos of political candidates.

results.title: Five Ways A.I. Could Be Regulated - The New York Times
results.description: Europe’s Risk-Based Law: The European Union’s A.I. Act, which is being negotiated on Wednesday, assigns regulations proportionate to the level of risk posed by an A.I. tool. The idea is to create a sliding scale of regulations aimed at putting the heaviest restrictions on the riskiest A.I.

results.title: Hochul Reaches Deal on A.I. Regulation in New York - The New York Times
results.description: Kathy Hochul of New York signed legislation on Friday that will establish new state rules governing the safe development of the most-advanced artificial intelligence models. The law, which state lawmakers originally approved in June, was the subject of intense lobbying before and after its passage by technology companies seeking to weaken its provisions. The companies wanted New York’s A.I. regulations to more closely match those recently adopted by California, and successfully pushed Ms.

results.title: E.U. Agrees on AI Act, Landmark Regulation for Artificial Intelligence - The New York Times
results.description: 8, 2023 · European Union policymakers agreed on Friday to a sweeping new law to regulate artificial intelligence, one of the world’s first comprehensive attempts to limit the use of a rapidly evolving technology that has wide-ranging societal ...

results.title: European Union Unveils Rules for Powerful A.I. Systems - The New York Times
results.description: Makers of the most advanced artificial intelligence systems face obligations for transparency, copyright protection and public safety. The rules are not enforceable until next year.

details.intent: After Article A and Article B are visited and their fields extracted, merge the outputs to produce a concise comparative summary describing similarities and differences in framing, recommended policy approaches, stakeholders emphasized, and tone.
inputs_required: Article A extracted fields (headline, date, main_point_summary, source, url, supporting_quote)
Article B extracted fields (headline, date, main_point_summary, source, url, supporting_quote)
details.synthesis_plan: 1) List each article's key facts (headline, date, source, main point). 2) Identify common themes (e.g., calls for regulation, focus on safety, economic concerns, national approaches). 3) Identify divergent viewpoints (e.g., strict vs flexible regulation, enforcement mechanisms, industry reaction). 4) Note differences in tone (alarmist, measured, policy-focused) and any notable quotes. 5) Produce a 3–4 sentence comparative summary plus 3 bullet points: commonalities, differences, and implications.
measurable_output_fields: article_summaries (array of objects with headline, date, source, main_point_summary, url)
verification_steps: Ensure facts (dates, headlines) match extracted values exactly.
Quote any contentious claims and attach source.
If either article is paywalled and missing data, note the limitation.
details.reasoning_and_rationale: A structured synthesis ensures the deliverable meets the mandate: extract required fields and provide a clear comparison of perspectives. The measurable outputs let downstream consumers verify and trace claims back to source URLs.
details.parent_goal: Search for recent news articles about 'artificial intelligence regulation' from the past year. Visit at least 2 different news websites (not Wikipedia) and extract: (1) The headline of each article, (2) The publication date, (3) The main topic or key point, (4) The source website name. Provide a summary comparing the different perspectives.

details.query: artificial intelligence regulation site:news OR site:nytimes.com OR site:reuters.com OR site:bbc.com OR site:theguardian.com OR site:washingtonpost.com "artificial intelligence regulation"
details.intent: Find recent (within the past 12 months) news articles about AI regulation from reputable news websites (exclude Wikipedia). Return top 6–8 results prioritized by relevance and recency.
details.extraction_plan: From each search result snippet capture the URL field, headline/snippet, site/domain, and any visible date. Prioritize articles from major news outlets (Reuters, BBC, NYT, Guardian, Washington Post, Bloomberg, Financial Times).
verification_steps: Confirm the article publication date is within the past 12 months from execution date.
Prefer articles that are clearly labeled as news/opinion and come from established news domains.
Check that the URL is direct to the article (not a topic hub) and accessible.
details.reasoning_and_rationale: A focused search will produce candidate article URLs to visit. Producing multiple candidate URLs reduces uncertainty and ensures we can visit at least two different news websites as required. Capturing the snippet and domain in search results helps select high-quality articles for deeper extraction.

Provide a summary comparing the different perspectives.

8b06da45-a921-40be-8bc6-1ccaec4dce25.title: Visit Article A — extract headline, date, main point, source (from search result 0)

details.intent: Open the top search result (index 0) and extract the required fields: headline, publication date, main topic/key point, and source website name.
details.extraction_plan: 1) Load the exact URL from search result index 0. 2) Locate the article headline (H1 or title meta tag). 3) Find the publication date/time on the page (byline or meta tags). 4) Read the first 2–4 paragraphs + the lede to identify the main topic or key point in 1–2 sentences. 5) Record the source domain and canonical URL. 6) Save an exact quote (1 sentence) that captures the article's framing for later verification.
verification_steps: Confirm the page domain matches the search snippet domain.
Confirm the publication date falls within the past 12 months.
If the page is paywalled, capture available metadata (headline, meta date) and note paywall status.
details.reasoning_and_rationale: Visiting the article is required to extract exact headline, date, and the article's main point. Using the top search result first increases likelihood of high relevance and recency. Captured quote supports later comparison of perspectives.

Provide a summary comparing the different perspectives.

931b86be-2ba8-495a-8221-95052f5ffb20.title: Visit Article B — extract headline, date, main point, source (from search result 1)

details.intent: Open the second search result (index 1) and extract the required fields: headline, publication date, main topic/key point, and source website name.
details.extraction_plan: 1) Load the exact URL from search result index 1. 2) Extract headline, publication date, and first 2–4 paragraphs to summarize the main point in 1–2 sentences. 3) Record source name and canonical URL. 4) Save a representative quote for verification.
verification_steps: Confirm the article's date is within the past 12 months.
Confirm source is a news website (not Wikipedia or blog) and differs from Article A's source to meet the 'at least 2 different news websites' requirement.
details.reasoning_and_rationale: Visiting a second, different news source ensures we capture multiple perspectives and satisfy the 'at least 2 different news websites' requirement. This reduces sampling bias and supports a comparative summary.
